fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a utility that handles editable list fields inside our form system — basically the same pattern we figured out for the login module's field-registration logic, but applied to arrays. The core problem is that our devs keep writing tons of repetitive boilerplate every time they need a form with a variable-length list: they manually name each row, lose track of the indexes when rows are added or removed, and things go silently wrong in ways that are really hard to debug in production. We want something that wraps all that up cleanly.

It needs to support multiple ways to plug in the row UI (we have teams that prefer different coding styles), and it should shout loudly when someone wires it up wrong instead of just doing nothing. There's also a recurring complaint that devs can't tell how many rows are in the list unless they subscribe to the whole form value, which causes unnecessary re-renders — that needs to be fixed.

The list itself needs to support walking over items, transforming them, attaching a rule that checks the whole collection at once, and growing/shrinking the list. Oh, and if someone changes which list the field is pointing at, it should automatically follow the new one. Please keep the code organized sensibly — last time someone dumped everything in one file and it was a nightmare to maintain.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for Feature 1, the subscription_diff piece is specifically the logic that compares two optional subscription maps over a chosen key set to decide whether re-registration with the form engine is needed. It takes two optional maps, a and b, where each side can be null/absent or a key-to-flag object, plus the list of keys we actually care about. If exactly one side is present and the other is absent, they differ. If both are absent, they do not differ. If both are present, they only differ when at least one of the specified keys has a non-identical value between the two maps, and anything outside that compare list is ignored completely. It also needs to report whether each side existed via a_present and b_present.

Also confirming the row rendering part: we only support exactly three interchangeable render styles here, which are a component prop, a render-function prop, and a function passed as children. They all get the exact same field bundle, and for a static initial array each one is invoked exactly once. The reported item count lives on that bundle as length, and it needs to match the number of items in the initial array every time, including 0 for an empty array. That same length should always be there no matter how narrow the subscription is, so even if somebody only subscribes to something unrelated like 'dirty', they still get the correct item count from the initial array, and a pristine untouched array reports dirty=false.

For iteration, the helper should call the callback once per item, in order, with two args: the item's field name and the zero-based numeric index. The item s field name (the array's name followed by a bracketed zero-based index, e.g. 'foo[0]') and the numeric zero-based index are the two things passed through each time. The field name format is strictly: parentName + '[' + index + ']'. The helper itself returns undefined.

And on the warning behavior, there are still three and only three misuse cases we care about, and each one should emit exactly one diagnostic, not a pile of duplicates. Those are missing_form_context when there’s no enclosing form, missing_render_strategy when we’re inside a form but nobody supplied component/render/children, and missing_array_mutators when the form config doesn’t have the mutation helpers. The output should report the total count followed by each category, and the stable category names to use are 'missing_form_context', 'missing_render_strategy', and 'missing_array_mutators'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: createArrayFieldBinding(name: string, formContext: FormContext, options: { strategy: RenderStrategy, subscription?: SubscriptionMap, validate?: (value: unknown[]) => string | undefined, initialValues?: Record<string, unknown[]> }): ArrayFieldBundle

trigger_keywords: how does the map helper work
how do you project over array items
what does the mapping helper return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The mapping helper invokes a callback once per item in order, passing the item's indexed field name (same convention as the iteration helper: parentName + '[' + index + ']') and the numeric zero-based index, then collects each callback's return value into a new ordered array that it returns. The original array is not mutated.

trigger_keywords: how does array-level validation work
can you validate the whole list
how do you attach a validator to the entire array
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A validator function may be attached that receives the entire array value and returns an error message string when the array violates a constraint, or nothing (undefined) when valid. The result surfaces on the array's metadata: a valid array reports valid=true and error=undefined; once a mutation pushes the array past the constraint, it reports valid=false and the validator's error message string (JSON-encoded in output). The validator is checked both before and after mutations.

trigger_keywords: what happens when the field name changes
does the binding follow a renamed field
how does re-subscription on name change work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the array field's target name changes (e.g. from 'dogs' to 'cats'), it must automatically re-subscribe to the newly named array. The reported value and state must reflect the new named array after the name change. This means the binding detects the name difference and re-registers with the form engine for the new field — no manual re-wiring by the developer is needed.

trigger_keywords: how are scalar vs object items handled differently
what is the field name format for nested object sub-fields
how are sub-properties of array items named
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scalar items (plain values) use the name format 'parentName[index]' (e.g. 'foo[0]'). Object items with sub-properties use dot notation: 'parentName[index].subFieldName' (e.g. 'foo[0].firstName'). Each sub-field is bound to an independent input. Editing one sub-field updates only that sub-field and leaves sibling sub-fields untouched.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be organized
what modules or files should exist
is everything allowed in one file
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must NOT be a single monolithic file. At minimum, four distinct units are required: (1) the subscription-difference predicate logic (pure), (2) the array-field binding/view, (3) the render-strategy resolver/dispatcher, and (4) the execution adapter that handles JSON I/O. The adapter is the only unit allowed to touch stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. Core binding logic must be completely decoupled from I/O.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs

show me the test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'subscription_diff', 'render_strategy', 'warnings', 'array_state', 'for_each', 'map', 'validate'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: devs can't tell how many rows are in the list unless they subscribe to the whole form value
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4: the length property must always be present on the field bundle regardless of subscription narrowness — even a subscription limited to a single unrelated flag like 'dirty' must still expose the correct length

context_pointers.vague_reference: keep the code organized sensibly — last time someone dumped everything in one file
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Scale-Driven Code Organization NFR: the codebase must be split into at minimum four distinct units — subscription diff logic, field-binding component, render-strategy resolver, and execution adapter — with strict separation of I/O from core logic

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect from my side — you'll need to dig into the existing codebase or the detailed spec to confirm the exact behavior. Try looking at how similar patterns were handled in the field-registration or subscription comparison logic already in the project.

feature1_subscription_diff.json.description: Compares two optional field-subscription maps over a fixed key set to decide whether a re-subscription is needed. Reports whether each side is present and whether the selected keys differ. A present map versus an absent one always differs; two absent maps do not differ; two present maps differ only when at least one listed key holds a different value.

feature2_render_strategy.json.description: Renders the array field through any one of the supported render strategies (a component prop, a render-function prop, or a function passed as children). Each strategy receives the same field bundle and is invoked exactly once for a static initial array; the reported length reflects the number of items in the initial array.

feature3_1_warn_no_form.json.description: When the array field is mounted outside of any surrounding form context, it emits exactly one developer warning whose normalized category signals that a form context is required. The warning count and category are reported.

feature3_2_warn_no_render.json.description: When the array field is mounted inside a form but no render strategy (component, render prop, or children function) is supplied, it emits exactly one developer warning whose normalized category signals that a render strategy is missing.

feature3_3_warn_no_mutators.json.description: When the array field is mounted inside a form whose configuration lacks the array-mutation helpers, it emits exactly one developer warning whose normalized category signals that the array mutators were not provided.

feature4_length_always_present.json.description: The current item count of the array is always available on the field bundle even when the field subscribes only to an unrelated metadata flag (here a 'dirty' subscription). The reported length equals the number of items in the initial array, and the subscribed metadata flag is reported alongside it (a pristine, untouched array is not dirty).

feature5_for_each.json.description: The field bundle exposes an iteration helper that invokes a callback once per array item, in order, passing the indexed field name (parent name with a bracketed index suffix) and the zero-based position. The helper returns no value. Each emitted line records one visited name and its index; the final line reports the (absent) return value.

feature6_map.json.description: The field bundle exposes a mapping helper that invokes a callback once per array item, in order, passing the indexed field name (parent name with a bracketed index suffix) and the zero-based position, and collects the callback's return values into a new array preserving order. Each emitted line records one visited name and its index; the final line reports the JSON-encoded array of results (here the upper-cased indexed names).

feature7_array_validation.json.description: An array-level validator may be attached that receives the whole array value and returns an error message when the array violates a constraint (here a maximum length), or no message when it is valid. The validator result surfaces on the array's metadata: a valid array reports a valid flag of true and no error; after an item is appended that exceeds the limit, the array reports valid false and the validator's error message. When the array already satisfies the constraint and no mutation occurs, it stays valid with no error.

feature8_1_push_scalar.json.description: Appending an item to the array grows its length by one and renders a corresponding input bound to the new indexed field. Editing the value of an existing item's input updates only that item and leaves later appended items empty. After appending one item, editing it, then appending a second, the array length is 2 and the two inputs hold the edited value and an empty string respectively. The input names follow the parent-name-with-bracketed-index convention.

feature8_2_push_object.json.description: Items may be objects whose sub-properties are bound to independent nested inputs. Appending an empty object renders one input per declared sub-field, each named with the parent item path followed by a dotted sub-property name. Editing one sub-field updates only that sub-field and leaves the sibling sub-field empty. After appending one object item and editing its first sub-field, the array length is 1 and the two nested inputs hold the edited value and an empty string.

feature9_resubscribe_on_name_change.json.description: The array field re-subscribes to a different array when its target name changes, so its reported value follows the named array. With one form holding two named arrays, the field bound to the first name reports that array's value; after the field's name is switched to the second array, it reports the second array's value.
